title: Napoleon's Last Gamble
subtitle: The Battle of Waterloo, June 18, 1815
tagline: He had been exiled. He came back with nothing and reclaimed France in three weeks. Now the entire continent stood against him. He had one chance. One battle. It lasted twelve hours.
villain_mandate: Napoleon at Waterloo must feel genuinely dangerous — not a beaten man making a desperate last stand, but the greatest military commander alive making a plan that almost works. Wellington must feel genuinely outnumbered and outclassed until the Prussians arrive. The margin of Napoleon's defeat must feel razor-thin.

scenes.title: The Return
scenes.narration: March 1815. Napoleon Bonaparte had been exiled to the island of Elba. He escaped with eleven hundred soldiers, landed in southern France, and marched north. The King sent an army to stop him. The army joined him instead. In three weeks, without a battle, he walked back to Paris and reclaimed his throne. The entire continent — Britain, Prussia, Austria, Russia — immediately declared war. He had perhaps ninety days before their combined armies reached France. He attacked first.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Napoleon landing at Golfe-Juan with eleven hundred soldiers — tiny, almost absurd. Then: French troops sent to stop him, meeting him on the road, officers shouting, then lowering their weapons, then joining him. The march to Paris gathering soldiers like a tide. Napoleon arriving at the Tuileries Palace. Then: Europe mobilizing against him, massive, inevitable. 16:9.

scenes.title: Divide and Destroy
scenes.narration: Napoleon's plan was his classic strategy: attack before the allies could unite. The allied forces were in two groups — Wellington's British and allied army in Belgium, Blücher's Prussian army to the east. Napoleon drove north with one hundred and twenty thousand soldiers, aiming to strike between them, defeat each separately before they could combine. On June 16, he hit Blücher at Ligny and won — but Blücher retreated north, not east. Not away from Wellington. Toward him.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Military map showing Napoleon's strategy: the allied armies shown in two groups, Napoleon's force driving between them. The Battle of Ligny: Blücher's Prussians defeated, forced back. Then: the critical map detail — Blücher retreating north, toward Wellington, not east away from him. Napoleon's staff missing this. The Prussians are not out of the battle. They are regrouping to fight again tomorrow. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Reverse Slope
scenes.narration: Wellington chose his ground carefully. A ridge south of the village of Waterloo. He positioned his soldiers on the reverse slope — the back side of the ridge, invisible to Napoleon. Napoleon would be firing artillery at empty ground. Wellington's troops, sheltered behind the ridge, would advance only when the French infantry climbed within musket range. His right flank anchored on the fortified farm of Hougoumont. He had sixty-eight thousand soldiers. He sent one message to Blücher: I need you by afternoon.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Wellington surveying his position at Mont-Saint-Jean ridge — calm, analytical, placing his soldiers deliberately. The reverse slope shown from Napoleon's perspective: an empty ridge, nothing visible. Then from Wellington's side: the entire British army sheltered, waiting. Hougoumont farm: fortified, garrisoned, the right flank anchor. Wellington's message to Blücher visible being sent. 16:9.

scenes.title: Napoleon Waits
scenes.narration: Napoleon delayed his attack until 11:30 AM — waiting for the ground to dry after overnight rain so his artillery could maneuver. His generals argued for an earlier attack. He refused. It was a reasonable decision. It was also fatal. Every hour he waited was another hour Blücher's Prussians had to march toward Wellington. Napoleon didn't know Blücher was coming. He had sent Grouchy's thirty-three thousand soldiers east to pursue the Prussians — and they were marching in the wrong direction.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Napoleon's delay — the overnight rain, the wet ground, the artillery unable to move until late morning. His generals arguing for earlier attack. Napoleon's decision to wait. Then: the Prussian march toward Waterloo — Blücher, sixty-eight years old and recovering from being ridden over by cavalry at Ligny, personally leading his army toward the battle. The clock. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Farm That Wouldn't Fall
scenes.narration: Hougoumont farm was supposed to be a diversion — a feint to draw Wellington's reserves. Napoleon's brother Jerome sent wave after wave of French infantry at the farm. It never fell. A handful of British and German defenders held it for eight hours. Jerome committed more and more men — men Napoleon needed elsewhere. By afternoon, twelve thousand French soldiers had been spent against one farm. Wellington committed only twenty-four hundred defenders. Hougoumont held.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Hougoumont farm under assault — French infantry storming the walls, the gate briefly breached and then slammed shut, defenders holding. The famous gate scene: French soldiers forcing the gate, a British soldier pulling it closed from inside, the gate locked. Wave after wave of French attacks repulsed. The farm burning but still held. 16:9.

scenes.title: Ney's Cavalry Charges
scenes.narration: Mid-afternoon. Marshal Ney saw British soldiers moving behind the ridge — actually moving wounded to the rear — and believed Wellington was retreating. Without orders, he launched twelve thousand heavy cavalry in a charge against the ridge. Wellington's infantry formed squares — the defensive formation that was virtually impenetrable to cavalry but completely vulnerable to artillery. Napoleon had the artillery to destroy the squares. He did not send it. The cavalry charges broke on the squares.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. The French cavalry charge — twelve thousand heavy cavalry, magnificent and terrifying, galloping toward the ridge. Wellington's infantry forming squares: tight formations bristling with bayonets. The cavalry crashing against the squares, unable to break them, wheeling away, charging again. Over and over. Then: the squares still standing. The cavalry exhausted. No artillery support sent. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Fourth Army
scenes.narration: Late afternoon. Wellington's line was breaking. Several regiments had fled. The French La Haye Sainte farmhouse at the center had fallen — a gap in Wellington's line exposed his center. Napoleon was preparing to commit the Imperial Guard — his elite, the undefeated — for the killing blow. Then, to the east: black shapes on the horizon. Blücher's Prussians. Fifty thousand soldiers who had marched since early morning. They arrived at Waterloo at 4:30 PM.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Wellington's line barely holding — regiments broken, gaps in the line, La Haye Sainte fallen, the center exposed. Napoleon preparing the Imperial Guard. Then: the Prussians appearing on the eastern horizon — dark mass, moving fast, unmistakable. Wellington receiving the news. Napoleon receiving the news. Their faces: relief and alarm, mirror images. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Guard Retreats
scenes.narration: Napoleon committed the Imperial Guard. They had never retreated. They had never been defeated. They had been the decisive weapon of fifty battles. They advanced up the ridge toward the British line. Wellington's reserves, positioned on the reverse slope where they were invisible, stood up and fired a volley at point-blank range. Then charged. The Imperial Guard — for the first time in their existence — broke. When the French army saw the Guard retreating, the rout began. 'La Garde recule!' The Guard retreats. The army dissolved.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. The Imperial Guard advancing — magnificent, unstoppable, the finest soldiers in Europe. The British reserves standing up from the reverse slope, invisible until now, volley at point-blank range. The charge into the Guard. The Guard breaking — the moment shown in close-up: the Guard falling back, the formation breaking. The French army watching. Then: rout. 'La Garde recule!' spreading through the French lines like fire. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Second Exile
scenes.narration: Napoleon fled the battlefield in his carriage. He reached Paris and attempted to raise another army. The political will was gone. He abdicated for the second time and surrendered to the British — specifically requesting British protection, addressing himself to 'the Prince Regent, the most powerful, the most constant, the most generous of my enemies.' He was sent to Saint Helena, an island in the south Atlantic, eight hundred miles from the nearest land. He died there six years later.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Napoleon's flight from Waterloo — leaving his carriage and mounting a horse to escape faster. Paris again, the abdication, the surrender to the British. Napoleon on HMS Bellerophon: surrounded by curious British sailors and officers, looking at England's coastline he will never land on. The ship to Saint Helena. The island: remote, volcanic, small. 16:9.

scenes.title: One Hundred Days
scenes.narration: From escape to exile: one hundred days. He escaped with eleven hundred men and walked back to power. He came within hours of defeating Wellington before the Prussians arrived. His delay of two hours in the morning cost him the battle. Grouchy chasing the wrong Prussian force cost him the war. Napoleon lost Waterloo not to Wellington — Wellington held the line. He lost it to the clock. Subscribe to Gods & Glory — the battles that ended empires.
scenes.visual_prompt: Epic historical documentary style. Final card — the battlefield of Waterloo today: the Lion's Mound, the farmhouse of Hougoumont still standing, the ridge of Mont-Saint-Jean. The quiet fields where twelve hours destroyed the most famous military career in history. The clock that beat Napoleon: shown as a simple sundial, moving through the morning hours of June 18, 1815. 16:9.

viral_hook: Napoleon escaped from exile with eleven hundred soldiers and walked back to power in three weeks. Then the entire continent declared war. He had ninety days. He almost made it.
highlight_note: Scene 8 — 'La Garde recule!' The Guard has never retreated. The moment the French army sees it and dissolves. The turning point of history visible in one phrase.
youtube_title: Napoleon's Last Gamble | The Battle of Waterloo | Gods & Glory
lesson: Napoleon lost Waterloo to two hours and one general chasing the wrong army. The greatest military mind in history was beaten by a clock and a mistake he couldn't fix in time.
